content.text: At each turn, the model reads the tools list and decides independently whether to call a tool or answer directly. This decision is not random — it is driven by the tool descriptions, the user's message, the conversation context, and any
override you provide. Understanding the decision process lets you design tools and prompts that produce the calling behaviour you need.

attrs.title: Input signals → Model decision → tool_use or end_turn

content.text: The model decides based on what you wrote in the description, not on the tool's internal logic.
If the description says 'use for X' and the user asks about Y, the model will not call the tool even if it technically could handle Y. The description is the model's only decision signal.
field in the response tells you what the model decided.
means the model chose to call one or more tools;
means it answered directly without tools:
Model wants to call one or more tools
Read content for tool_use blocks, execute each, send tool_result
Read text block from content — this is the final answer
Increase max_tokens or detect and handle partial response
How Tool Descriptions Drive the Decision

The model reads tool descriptions at inference time and matches them to the user's need. A vague description produces inconsistent calling; a precise description that states when to use the tool produces reliable calling:
State explicitly when the model MUST call the tool: 'Use this before answering any question about ...'
Disambiguate from similar tools: 'Use this instead of search_web when the question is about internal policy'
State what the tool cannot do: 'Only works for US stock tickers'
When you need deterministic calling behaviour — for structured output extraction, guaranteed tool execution, or testing — use the
parameter to override the model's autonomous decision:

# Force the model to call a specific tool on this turn
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "search_knowledge_base"},
    messages=messages
)
# stop_reason is always "tool_use" with this setting

# Force the model to call ANY tool (but let it pick which one)
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "any"},   # model must call at least one tool
    messages=messages
)

# Let the model decide (default)
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
    messages=messages
)
is useful when you have multiple tools and want to guarantee at least one is called, but are willing to let the model pick which. It is more flexible than
(which locks in a specific tool) and more constrained than

The system prompt can instruct the model when to call tools. Explicit policies in the system prompt are more reliable than relying solely on tool descriptions, especially when you need to override the model's default judgement:

# System prompt instructions influence tool-calling behaviour

system_with_policy = """You are a customer support assistant for Acme Corp.

Tool use policy:
- Always call search_knowledge_base before answering questions about products,
  pricing, returns, or shipping. Never answer these from memory.
- Call create_ticket when the user reports a bug or requests a feature.
- Do not call any tool for general greetings or questions unrelated to Acme products.
"""

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=system_with_policy,
    tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is your return policy?"}]
)
# Model will call search_knowledge_base because the system prompt mandates it

System-prompt tool policies work best when they are unambiguous and tied to clear trigger conditions. Avoid vague instructions like 'use tools when helpful' — instead specify the exact question categories or signals that should trigger each tool.
When the model determines that multiple tools are needed and their calls are independent, it returns several
blocks in a single response. This happens without any special configuration — the model decides when parallel calls are appropriate:
Your dispatch loop already handles this: iterate
block, and return all results in a single user message. The results can be executed concurrently in your application if the tools support it (e.g., using
When the model skips a tool call you expected, or calls the wrong tool, these diagnostic steps identify the cause:

# If the model is not calling a tool when you expect it to,
# check these common causes:

# 1. Log the stop_reason and content to see what the model returned
print("stop_reason:", response.stop_reason)
for block in response.content:
    print("block:", block.type, getattr(block, "name", ""), getattr(block, "text", "")[:80])

# 2. Inspect which tools were sent
print("tools sent:", [t["name"] for t in tools])

# 3. Try forcing the call to confirm the tool works at all
forced = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=512,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "your_tool_name"},
    messages=messages
)
print("forced call input:", forced.content[0].input)

# 4. Print the full description the model is reading
for tool in tools:
    print(f"--- {tool['name']} ---")
    print(tool["description"])
Log stop_reason and content blocks to see exactly what the model returned
Confirm the tool is in the tools list sent to the API (not just defined in your code)
Force the call with tool_choice to verify the tool input schema works
Reread the description — if it is ambiguous, the model may not recognise when to use it
Check the system prompt for conflicting instructions that discourage tool use
blocks. The model sometimes reasons out loud before calling a tool. Always iterate all blocks rather than assuming the first is the only one:

# When stop_reason is "tool_use", the content list may contain both
# a text block AND tool_use blocks — the model can reason out loud
# before calling a tool.

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the stock price of Apple?"}]
)

for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "text":
        print("Reasoning:", block.text)  # "Let me look up the current price..."
    elif block.type == "tool_use":
        print("Tool call:", block.name, block.input)  # get_stock_price {"ticker": "AAPL"}

# Always process ALL blocks in content, not just the first one.
# A response can have:
# - [text, tool_use]   — reasoning + tool call
# - [tool_use]         — direct call, no reasoning
# - [tool_use, tool_use] — parallel calls
# - [text]             — end_turn, no tool
Direct tool call, no reasoning text
Model reasoned, then decided to call
Parallel calls — two tools requested simultaneously
end_turn — model answered without any tool call

You can empirically measure how often a tool description triggers a call versus is skipped. Run a small test set of known-trigger and known-skip messages and compare recall between description variants:

# A/B test: which description produces more reliable tool calls?

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TEST_MESSAGES = [
    "How much does the Pro plan cost?",
    "Do you offer a free trial?",
    "What is included in the Enterprise plan?",
    "Hello, how are you?",                  # should NOT trigger the tool
    "What is the capital of France?",        # should NOT trigger the tool
]

def measure_recall(tool_description: str) -> dict:
    """Run test messages and count how often the tool is called vs skipped."""
    called = 0
    skipped = 0
    for msg in TEST_MESSAGES[:3]:  # first 3 should trigger
        r = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=256,
            tools=[{"name": "search_pricing", "description": tool_description,
                    "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                                     "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
                                     "required": ["query"]}}],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": msg}]
        )
        if r.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            called += 1
        else:
            skipped += 1
    return {"called": called, "skipped": skipped, "recall": called / 3}

vague_desc = "Searches for pricing information."
precise_desc = (
    "Search the pricing database for plan costs, features, and trial information. "
    "Always use this when the user asks about pricing, plans, costs, or free trials."
)

print("Vague:  ", measure_recall(vague_desc))
print("Precise:", measure_recall(precise_desc))
# Typical output:
# Vague:   {'called': 1, 'skipped': 2, 'recall': 0.33}
# Precise: {'called': 3, 'skipped': 0, 'recall': 1.0}

Run this test whenever you revise a tool description. A recall below 0.8 on trigger messages usually indicates the description is too vague. A false-positive rate above 0.1 on non-trigger messages usually indicates the description is too broad.

list on each API call gives you precise control over which tools the model can reach for at each stage of a conversation. Omit tools that are not relevant to the current phase to prevent unnecessary calls:

# Pattern: omit tools from specific turns to prevent unnecessary calls.
# Pass different tools lists depending on the phase of the conversation.

def get_tools_for_phase(phase: str) -> list:
    ALL_TOOLS = [search_tool, create_ticket_tool, send_email_tool]
    READ_ONLY = [search_tool]
    NONE = []

    phases = {
        "initial":       READ_ONLY,    # only allow search at the start
        "information":   ALL_TOOLS,    # all tools once context is established
        "confirmation":  NONE,         # force text-only confirmation step
        "complete":      NONE,
    }
    return phases.get(phase, ALL_TOOLS)

# Use in the loop
tools_for_turn = get_tools_for_phase(current_phase)
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools_for_turn if tools_for_turn else None,  # None = no tools
    messages=messages
)
Pass tools=None (or omit the parameter) to fully disable tool use for a turn
Restricting to read-only tools during initial turns prevents premature mutations
A confirmation phase with no tools forces the model to ask the user rather than act
Tool availability changes are invisible to the model — it only sees what is in the current call
The model decides to call a tool when its description matches the user's need and external data or an action is required. The decision is visible in
means the model chose to call,
means it answered directly. Description clarity is the primary control — precise 'use this when ...' language produces reliable calling. The
parameter overrides autonomous decisions when deterministic behaviour is required. The model may call multiple tools in a single turn when goals are independent, returning parallel
